scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 5 anti-patterns identified by name (shared database, synchronous fan-in, god endpoint, REST-as-job-runner, direct cross-service DB reads). Prioritized remediation plan with 4 phases sequenced by blast radius. Shared database and direct DB reads identified as highest risk (Phase 1/2). REST-based jobs identified as fragile with HTTP timeout and re-entrancy concerns. Trade-offs for fixing the shared database explicitly included (cross-service queries become harder, data sync mechanism needed, significant migration effort). Incremental remediation approach via phased plan, not big bang rewrite.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough identification of all 5 anti-patterns with clear explanations of why each is harmful
Prioritization is well-sequenced: database coupling first, then communication patterns, then API design — matches expected risk ordering
Phase 1 'stop the bleeding' tactical mitigations (circuit breakers, idempotency keys, DB views) show practical incremental thinking
Trade-offs section covers all 5 anti-patterns with gains, sacrifices, and alternatives considered
Pitfalls section adds valuable practical guidance (e.g., don't migrate all Notification callers at once, don't add ?fields= to god endpoint)
Domain model section with context map showing current vs desired relationships adds useful framing
Timeline estimates (weeks 1-3, 4-10, etc.) were provided despite being speculative — though this is minor since the sequencing itself is sound

agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The shared database between Order and Courier services is the most critical issue — it creates invisible coupling that makes independent deployment impossible and risks cascading failures. The synchronous Notification fan-in, the god endpoint on Customer Service, REST-as-cron job execution, and direct database reads by Reporting each compound the fragility

trials.justification: All 5 anti-patterns identified by name (shared DB, synchronous Notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-as-jobs, direct cross-service DB reads). Remediation is clearly prioritized into 3 waves with shared DB and direct DB reads as highest risk. REST-based jobs explicitly called fragile. Trade-offs for fixing shared database are detailed (migration cost, dual-write, eventual consistency). Incremental remediation via Strangler Fig pattern, not big-bang rewrite.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns clearly identified with specific explanations of why each is harmful
Three-wave prioritized remediation plan with Wave 1 stabilization (circuit breakers, read replica) before Wave 2-3 structural changes
Shared DB and direct DB reads correctly identified as highest risk items blocking safe deployment
REST-based jobs explicitly called out for HTTP timeout fragility and re-entrancy risk
Comprehensive trade-offs for each remediation with alternatives considered
Explicit pitfalls section warns against big-bang rewrite and other common mistakes
Strangler Fig pattern recommended for incremental database split — exactly the right approach
Security concern flagged for god endpoint leaking payment data (PCI exposure)

trials.agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). The synchronous Notification fan-in creates a cascading failure risk across all five callers. The god endpoint on Customer Service violates interface segregation

trials.justification: All 5 anti-patterns identified by name (shared DB, sync notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-as-cron-jobs, direct cross-service DB reads). Prioritized remediation in 3 phases with shared DB and notification bottleneck as Phase 1 (highest risk), Reporting DB decoupling as Phase 2, god endpoint and job infra as Phase 3. REST-based jobs explicitly called out as fragile. Trade-offs for shared DB split are detailed (migration downtime, dual-write, eventual consistency). Approach is incremental (Strangler Fig, phased rollout) not big-bang rewrite.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns clearly identified with precise explanations of why each is harmful
Prioritization is well-reasoned: shared DB and notification cascade as critical/Phase 1, direct DB reads as high/Phase 2, god endpoint and cron jobs as medium/Phase 3
Trade-offs section is exceptionally thorough with alternatives considered for each remediation
Incremental remediation with Strangler Fig pattern, dual-write migration, and explicit pitfalls to avoid
Context map using DDD terminology (Shared Kernel, Conformist, Published Language) adds precision to the analysis
Concrete interim mitigations (DB permission grants, circuit breakers) provided alongside strategic fixes
